name: asyncio
documentation:
**This version is only relevant for Python 3.3, which does not include asyncio in its stdlib.**

Master repo: <https://github.com/python/asyncio>

The asyncio module provides infrastructure for writing single-threaded
concurrent code using coroutines, multiplexing I/O access over sockets and
other resources, running network clients and servers, and other related
primitives.  Here is a more detailed list of the package contents:

- a pluggable event loop with various system-specific implementations;
- transport and protocol abstractions (similar to those in Twisted);
- concrete support for TCP, UDP, SSL, subprocess pipes, delayed calls, and
  others (some may be system-dependent);
- a Future class that mimics the one in the concurrent.futures module, but
  adapted for use with the event loop;
- coroutines and tasks based on `yield from` (PEP 380), to help write
  concurrent code in a sequential fashion;
- cancellation support for Futures and coroutines;
- synchronization primitives for use between coroutines in a single thread,
  mimicking those in the threading module;
- an interface for passing work off to a threadpool, for times when you
  absolutely, positively have to use a library that makes blocking I/O calls.

Note: The implementation of asyncio was previously called "Tulip".

# Installation

To install asyncio, type:

```
pip install asyncio
```

asyncio requires Python 3.3 or later! The asyncio module is part of the Python
standard library since Python 3.4.

asyncio is a free software distributed under the Apache license version 2.0.

# Websites

- [asyncio project at GitHub](https://github.com/python/asyncio): source
  code, bug tracker
- [asyncio documentation](https://docs.python.org/dev/library/asyncio.html)
- Mailing list: [python-tulip Google Group](https://groups.google.com/forum/?fromgroups#!forum/python-tulip)
- IRC: join the `#asyncio` channel on the Freenode network

# Development

The actual code lives in the 'asyncio' subdirectory. Tests are in the 'tests'
subdirectory.

To run tests, run:

```
tox
```

Or use the Makefile:

```
make test
```

To run coverage (coverage package is required):

```
make coverage
```

On Windows, things are a little more complicated.  Assume 'P' is your
Python binary (for example C:Python33python.exe).

You must first build the \_overlapped.pyd extension and have it placed
in the asyncio directory, as follows:

> C> P setup.py build_ext --inplace

If this complains about vcvars.bat, you probably don't have the
required version of Visual Studio installed.  Compiling extensions for
Python 3.3 requires Microsoft Visual C++ 2010 (MSVC 10.0) of any
edition; you can download Visual Studio Express 2010 for free from
<http://www.visualstudio.com/downloads> (scroll down to Visual C++ 2010
Express).

Once you have built the \_overlapped.pyd extension successfully you can
run the tests as follows:

> C> P runtests.py

And coverage as follows:

> C> P runtests.py --coverage

modules.name: asyncio.futures
documentation: A Future class similar to the one in PEP 3148.

functions.name: asyncio.futures.wrap_future
documentation: Wrap concurrent.futures.Future object.

variables.name: asyncio.futures.STACK_DEBUG
documentation: heavy-duty debugging

classes.name: asyncio.futures.InvalidStateError
documentation: The operation is not allowed in this state.

classes.name: asyncio.futures._TracebackLogger
documentation:
Helper to log a traceback upon destruction if not cleared.

This solves a nasty problem with Futures and Tasks that have an
exception set: if nobody asks for the exception, the exception is
never logged.  This violates the Zen of Python: 'Errors should
never pass silently.  Unless explicitly silenced.'

However, we don't want to log the exception as soon as
set_exception() is called: if the calling code is written
properly, it will get the exception and handle it properly.  But
we *do* want to log it if result() or exception() was never called
-- otherwise developers waste a lot of time wondering why their
buggy code fails silently.

An earlier attempt added a __del__() method to the Future class
itself, but this backfired because the presence of __del__()
prevents garbage collection from breaking cycles.  A way out of
this catch-22 is to avoid having a __del__() method on the Future
class itself, but instead to have a reference to a helper object
with a __del__() method that logs the traceback, where we ensure
that the helper object doesn't participate in cycles, and only the
Future has a reference to it.

The helper object is added when set_exception() is called.  When
the Future is collected, and the helper is present, the helper
object is also collected, and its __del__() method will log the
traceback.  When the Future's result() or exception() method is
called (and a helper object is present), it removes the helper
object, after calling its clear() method to prevent it from
logging.

One downside is that we do a fair amount of work to extract the
traceback from the exception, even when it is never logged.  It
would seem cheaper to just store the exception object, but that
references the traceback, which references stack frames, which may
reference the Future, which references the _TracebackLogger, and
then the _TracebackLogger would be included in a cycle, which is
what we're trying to avoid!  As an optimization, we don't
immediately format the exception; we only do the work when
activate() is called, which call is delayed until after all the
Future's callbacks have run.  Since usually a Future has at least
one callback (typically set by 'yield from') and usually that
callback extracts the callback, thereby removing the need to
format the exception.

PS. I don't claim credit for this solution.  I first heard of it
in a discussion about closing files when they are collected.

classes.name: asyncio.futures.Future

methods.name: asyncio.futures.Future.__init__
documentation:
Initialize the future.

The optional event_loop argument allows to explicitly set the event
loop object used by the future. If it's not provided, the future uses
the default event loop.

methods.name: asyncio.futures.Future.cancel
documentation:
Cancel the future and schedule callbacks.

If the future is already done or cancelled, return False.  Otherwise,
change the future's state to cancelled, schedule the callbacks and
return True.

methods.name: asyncio.futures.Future._schedule_callbacks
documentation:
Internal: Ask the event loop to call all callbacks.

The callbacks are scheduled to be called as soon as possible. Also
clears the callback list.

methods.name: asyncio.futures.Future.cancelled
documentation: Return True if the future was cancelled.

methods.name: asyncio.futures.Future.done
documentation:
Return True if the future is done.

Done means either that a result / exception are available, or that the
future was cancelled.
Don't implement running(); see http://bugs.python.org/issue18699

methods.name: asyncio.futures.Future.result
documentation:
Return the result this future represents.

If the future has been cancelled, raises CancelledError.  If the
future's result isn't yet available, raises InvalidStateError.  If
the future is done and has an exception set, this exception is raised.

methods.name: asyncio.futures.Future.exception
documentation:
Return the exception that was set on this future.

The exception (or None if no exception was set) is returned only if
the future is done.  If the future has been cancelled, raises
CancelledError.  If the future isn't done yet, raises
InvalidStateError.

methods.name: asyncio.futures.Future.add_done_callback
documentation:
Add a callback to be run when the future becomes done.

The callback is called with a single argument - the future object. If
the future is already done when this is called, the callback is
scheduled with call_soon.

methods.name: asyncio.futures.Future.remove_done_callback
documentation:
Remove all instances of a callback from the "call when done" list.

Returns the number of callbacks removed.
New method not in PEP 3148.

methods.name: asyncio.futures.Future._set_result_unless_cancelled
documentation: Helper setting the result only if the future was not cancelled.
So-called internal methods (note: no set_running_or_notify_cancel()).

methods.name: asyncio.futures.Future.set_result
documentation:
Mark the future done and set its result.

If the future is already done when this method is called, raises
InvalidStateError.

methods.name: asyncio.futures.Future.set_exception
documentation:
Mark the future done and set an exception.

If the future is already done when this method is called, raises
InvalidStateError.

methods.name: asyncio.futures.Future._copy_state
documentation:
Internal helper to copy state from another Future.

The other Future may be a concurrent.futures.Future.
Truly internal methods.

class_variables.name: asyncio.futures.Future._blocking
documentation: proper use of future (yield vs yield from)

class_variables.name: asyncio.futures.Future._log_traceback
documentation: Used for Python 3.4 and later

class_variables.name: asyncio.futures.Future._tb_logger
documentation: Used for Python 3.3 only

This class is *almost* compatible with concurrent.futures.Future.

Differences:

- result() and exception() do not take a timeout argument and
  raise an exception when the future isn't done yet.

- Callbacks registered with add_done_callback() are always called
  via the event loop's call_soon_threadsafe().

- This class is not compatible with the wait() and as_completed()
  methods in the concurrent.futures package.

(In Python 3.4 or later we may be able to unify the implementations.)

modules.name: asyncio.events
documentation: Event loop and event loop policy.

functions.name: asyncio.events._format_args
documentation:
Format function arguments.

Special case for a single parameter: ('hello',) is formatted as ('hello').

functions.name: asyncio.events.get_event_loop_policy
documentation: Get the current event loop policy.

functions.name: asyncio.events.set_event_loop_policy
documentation:
Set the current event loop policy.

If policy is None, the default policy is restored.

functions.name: asyncio.events.get_event_loop
documentation: Equivalent to calling get_event_loop_policy().get_event_loop().

functions.name: asyncio.events.set_event_loop
documentation: Equivalent to calling get_event_loop_policy().set_event_loop(loop).

functions.name: asyncio.events.new_event_loop
documentation: Equivalent to calling get_event_loop_policy().new_event_loop().

functions.name: asyncio.events.get_child_watcher
documentation: Equivalent to calling get_event_loop_policy().get_child_watcher().

functions.name: asyncio.events.set_child_watcher
documentation:
Equivalent to calling
get_event_loop_policy().set_child_watcher(watcher).

classes.name: asyncio.events.Handle
documentation: Object returned by callback registration methods.

classes.name: asyncio.events.TimerHandle
documentation: Object returned by timed callback registration methods.

classes.name: asyncio.events.AbstractServer

methods.name: asyncio.events.AbstractServer.close
documentation: Stop serving.  This leaves existing connections open.

methods.name: asyncio.events.AbstractServer.wait_closed
documentation: Coroutine to wait until service is closed.
Abstract server returned by create_server().

classes.name: asyncio.events.AbstractEventLoop

methods.name: asyncio.events.AbstractEventLoop.run_forever
documentation: Run the event loop until stop() is called.
Running and stopping the event loop.

methods.name: asyncio.events.AbstractEventLoop.run_until_complete
documentation:
Run the event loop until a Future is done.

Return the Future's result, or raise its exception.

methods.name: asyncio.events.AbstractEventLoop.stop
documentation:
Stop the event loop as soon as reasonable.

Exactly how soon that is may depend on the implementation, but
no more I/O callbacks should be scheduled.

methods.name: asyncio.events.AbstractEventLoop.is_running
documentation: Return whether the event loop is currently running.

methods.name: asyncio.events.AbstractEventLoop.is_closed
documentation: Returns True if the event loop was closed.

methods.name: asyncio.events.AbstractEventLoop.close
documentation:
Close the loop.

The loop should not be running.

This is idempotent and irreversible.

No other methods should be called after this one.

methods.name: asyncio.events.AbstractEventLoop._timer_handle_cancelled
documentation: Notification that a TimerHandle has been cancelled.
Methods scheduling callbacks.  All these return Handles.

methods.name: asyncio.events.AbstractEventLoop.create_task
documentation: Method scheduling a coroutine object: create a task.

methods.name: asyncio.events.AbstractEventLoop.call_soon_threadsafe
documentation: Methods for interacting with threads.

methods.name: asyncio.events.AbstractEventLoop.getaddrinfo
documentation: Network I/O methods returning Futures.

methods.name: asyncio.events.AbstractEventLoop.create_server
documentation:
A coroutine which creates a TCP server bound to host and port.

The return value is a Server object which can be used to stop
the service.

If host is an empty string or None all interfaces are assumed
and a list of multiple sockets will be returned (most likely
one for IPv4 and another one for IPv6).

family can be set to either AF_INET or AF_INET6 to force the
socket to use IPv4 or IPv6. If not set it will be determined
from host (defaults to AF_UNSPEC).

flags is a bitmask for getaddrinfo().

sock can optionally be specified in order to use a preexisting
socket object.

backlog is the maximum number of queued connections passed to
listen() (defaults to 100).

ssl can be set to an SSLContext to enable SSL over the
accepted connections.

reuse_address tells the kernel to reuse a local socket in
TIME_WAIT state, without waiting for its natural timeout to
expire. If not specified will automatically be set to True on
UNIX.

methods.name: asyncio.events.AbstractEventLoop.create_unix_server
documentation:
A coroutine which creates a UNIX Domain Socket server.

The return value is a Server object, which can be used to stop
the service.

path is a str, representing a file systsem path to bind the
server socket to.

sock can optionally be specified in order to use a preexisting
socket object.

backlog is the maximum number of queued connections passed to
listen() (defaults to 100).

ssl can be set to an SSLContext to enable SSL over the
accepted connections.

methods.name: asyncio.events.AbstractEventLoop.connect_read_pipe
documentation:
Register read pipe in event loop. Set the pipe to non-blocking mode.

protocol_factory should instantiate object with Protocol interface.
pipe is a file-like object.
Return pair (transport, protocol), where transport supports the
ReadTransport interface.
Pipes and subprocesses.

methods.name: asyncio.events.AbstractEventLoop.connect_write_pipe
documentation:
Register write pipe in event loop.

protocol_factory should instantiate object with BaseProtocol interface.
Pipe is file-like object already switched to nonblocking.
Return pair (transport, protocol), where transport support
WriteTransport interface.

methods.name: asyncio.events.AbstractEventLoop.add_reader
documentation: Ready-based callback registration methods.
The add_*() methods return None.
The remove_*() methods return True if something was removed,
False if there was nothing to delete.

methods.name: asyncio.events.AbstractEventLoop.sock_recv
documentation: Completion based I/O methods returning Futures.

methods.name: asyncio.events.AbstractEventLoop.add_signal_handler
documentation: Signal handling.

methods.name: asyncio.events.AbstractEventLoop.set_exception_handler
documentation: Error handlers.

methods.name: asyncio.events.AbstractEventLoop.get_debug
documentation: Debug flag management.
Abstract event loop.

classes.name: asyncio.events.AbstractEventLoopPolicy

methods.name: asyncio.events.AbstractEventLoopPolicy.get_event_loop
documentation:
Get the event loop for the current context.

Returns an event loop object implementing the BaseEventLoop interface,
or raises an exception in case no event loop has been set for the
current context and the current policy does not specify to create one.

It should never return None.

methods.name: asyncio.events.AbstractEventLoopPolicy.set_event_loop
documentation: Set the event loop for the current context to loop.

methods.name: asyncio.events.AbstractEventLoopPolicy.new_event_loop
documentation:
Create and return a new event loop object according to this
policy's rules. If there's need to set this loop as the event loop for
the current context, set_event_loop must be called explicitly.

methods.name: asyncio.events.AbstractEventLoopPolicy.get_child_watcher
documentation: Child processes handling (Unix only).

methods.name: asyncio.events.AbstractEventLoopPolicy.set_child_watcher
documentation: Set the watcher for child processes.
Abstract policy for accessing the event loop.

classes.name: asyncio.events.BaseDefaultEventLoopPolicy

methods.name: asyncio.events.BaseDefaultEventLoopPolicy.get_event_loop
documentation:
Get the event loop.

This may be None or an instance of EventLoop.

methods.name: asyncio.events.BaseDefaultEventLoopPolicy.set_event_loop
documentation: Set the event loop.

methods.name: asyncio.events.BaseDefaultEventLoopPolicy.new_event_loop
documentation:
Create a new event loop.

You must call set_event_loop() to make this the current event
loop.

Default policy implementation for accessing the event loop.

In this policy, each thread has its own event loop.  However, we
only automatically create an event loop by default for the main
thread; other threads by default have no event loop.

Other policies may have different rules (e.g. a single global
event loop, or automatically creating an event loop per thread, or
using some other notion of context to which an event loop is
associated).

modules.name: asyncio.locks
documentation: Synchronization primitives.

classes.name: asyncio.locks._ContextManager
documentation:
Context manager.

This enables the following idiom for acquiring and releasing a
lock around a block:

    with (yield from lock):
        <block>

while failing loudly when accidentally using:

    with lock:
        <block>

classes.name: asyncio.locks.Lock

methods.name: asyncio.locks.Lock.locked
documentation: Return True if lock is acquired.

methods.name: asyncio.locks.Lock.acquire
documentation:
Acquire a lock.

This method blocks until the lock is unlocked, then sets it to
locked and returns True.

methods.name: asyncio.locks.Lock.release
documentation:
Release a lock.

When the lock is locked, reset it to unlocked, and return.
If any other coroutines are blocked waiting for the lock to become
unlocked, allow exactly one of them to proceed.

When invoked on an unlocked lock, a RuntimeError is raised.

There is no return value.

Primitive lock objects.

A primitive lock is a synchronization primitive that is not owned
by a particular coroutine when locked.  A primitive lock is in one
of two states, 'locked' or 'unlocked'.

It is created in the unlocked state.  It has two basic methods,
acquire() and release().  When the state is unlocked, acquire()
changes the state to locked and returns immediately.  When the
state is locked, acquire() blocks until a call to release() in
another coroutine changes it to unlocked, then the acquire() call
resets it to locked and returns.  The release() method should only
be called in the locked state; it changes the state to unlocked
and returns immediately.  If an attempt is made to release an
unlocked lock, a RuntimeError will be raised.

When more than one coroutine is blocked in acquire() waiting for
the state to turn to unlocked, only one coroutine proceeds when a
release() call resets the state to unlocked; first coroutine which
is blocked in acquire() is being processed.

acquire() is a coroutine and should be called with 'yield from'.

Locks also support the context management protocol.  '(yield from lock)'
should be used as context manager expression.

Usage:

    lock = Lock()
    ...
    yield from lock
    try:
        ...
    finally:
        lock.release()

Context manager usage:

    lock = Lock()
    ...
    with (yield from lock):
         ...

Lock objects can be tested for locking state:

    if not lock.locked():
       yield from lock
    else:
       # lock is acquired
       ...

classes.name: asyncio.locks.Event

methods.name: asyncio.locks.Event.is_set
documentation: Return True if and only if the internal flag is true.

methods.name: asyncio.locks.Event.set
documentation:
Set the internal flag to true. All coroutines waiting for it to
become true are awakened. Coroutine that call wait() once the flag is
true will not block at all.

methods.name: asyncio.locks.Event.clear
documentation:
Reset the internal flag to false. Subsequently, coroutines calling
wait() will block until set() is called to set the internal flag
to true again.

methods.name: asyncio.locks.Event.wait
documentation:
Block until the internal flag is true.

If the internal flag is true on entry, return True
immediately.  Otherwise, block until another coroutine calls
set() to set the flag to true, then return True.

Asynchronous equivalent to threading.Event.

Class implementing event objects. An event manages a flag that can be set
to true with the set() method and reset to false with the clear() method.
The wait() method blocks until the flag is true. The flag is initially
false.

classes.name: asyncio.locks.Condition

methods.name: asyncio.locks.Condition.wait
documentation:
Wait until notified.

If the calling coroutine has not acquired the lock when this
method is called, a RuntimeError is raised.

This method releases the underlying lock, and then blocks
until it is awakened by a notify() or notify_all() call for
the same condition variable in another coroutine.  Once
awakened, it re-acquires the lock and returns True.

methods.name: asyncio.locks.Condition.wait_for
documentation:
Wait until a predicate becomes true.

The predicate should be a callable which result will be
interpreted as a boolean value.  The final predicate value is
the return value.

methods.name: asyncio.locks.Condition.notify
documentation:
By default, wake up one coroutine waiting on this condition, if any.
If the calling coroutine has not acquired the lock when this method
is called, a RuntimeError is raised.

This method wakes up at most n of the coroutines waiting for the
condition variable; it is a no-op if no coroutines are waiting.

Note: an awakened coroutine does not actually return from its
wait() call until it can reacquire the lock. Since notify() does
not release the lock, its caller should.

methods.name: asyncio.locks.Condition.notify_all
documentation:
Wake up all threads waiting on this condition. This method acts
like notify(), but wakes up all waiting threads instead of one. If the
calling thread has not acquired the lock when this method is called,
a RuntimeError is raised.

Asynchronous equivalent to threading.Condition.

This class implements condition variable objects. A condition variable
allows one or more coroutines to wait until they are notified by another
coroutine.

A new Lock object is created and used as the underlying lock.

classes.name: asyncio.locks.Semaphore

methods.name: asyncio.locks.Semaphore.locked
documentation: Returns True if semaphore can not be acquired immediately.

methods.name: asyncio.locks.Semaphore.acquire
documentation:
Acquire a semaphore.

If the internal counter is larger than zero on entry,
decrement it by one and return True immediately.  If it is
zero on entry, block, waiting until some other coroutine has
called release() to make it larger than 0, and then return
True.

methods.name: asyncio.locks.Semaphore.release
documentation:
Release a semaphore, incrementing the internal counter by one.
When it was zero on entry and another coroutine is waiting for it to
become larger than zero again, wake up that coroutine.

A Semaphore implementation.

A semaphore manages an internal counter which is decremented by each
acquire() call and incremented by each release() call. The counter
can never go below zero; when acquire() finds that it is zero, it blocks,
waiting until some other thread calls release().

Semaphores also support the context management protocol.

The optional argument gives the initial value for the internal
counter; it defaults to 1. If the value given is less than 0,
ValueError is raised.

classes.name: asyncio.locks.BoundedSemaphore
documentation:
A bounded semaphore implementation.

This raises ValueError in release() if it would increase the value
above the initial value.

modules.name: asyncio.streams
documentation: Stream-related things.

functions.name: asyncio.streams.open_connection
documentation:
A wrapper for create_connection() returning a (reader, writer) pair.

The reader returned is a StreamReader instance; the writer is a
StreamWriter instance.

The arguments are all the usual arguments to create_connection()
except protocol_factory; most common are positional host and port,
with various optional keyword arguments following.

Additional optional keyword arguments are loop (to set the event loop
instance to use) and limit (to set the buffer limit passed to the
StreamReader).

(If you want to customize the StreamReader and/or
StreamReaderProtocol classes, just copy the code -- there's
really nothing special here except some convenience.)

functions.name: asyncio.streams.start_server
documentation:
Start a socket server, call back for each client connected.

The first parameter, `client_connected_cb`, takes two parameters:
client_reader, client_writer.  client_reader is a StreamReader
object, while client_writer is a StreamWriter object.  This
parameter can either be a plain callback function or a coroutine;
if it is a coroutine, it will be automatically converted into a
Task.

The rest of the arguments are all the usual arguments to
loop.create_server() except protocol_factory; most common are
positional host and port, with various optional keyword arguments
following.  The return value is the same as loop.create_server().

Additional optional keyword arguments are loop (to set the event loop
instance to use) and limit (to set the buffer limit passed to the
StreamReader).

The return value is the same as loop.create_server(), i.e. a
Server object which can be used to stop the service.

functions.name: asyncio.streams.open_unix_connection
documentation: Similar to `open_connection` but works with UNIX Domain Sockets.
UNIX Domain Sockets are supported on this platform

functions.name: asyncio.streams.start_unix_server
documentation: Similar to `start_server` but works with UNIX Domain Sockets.

classes.name: asyncio.streams.IncompleteReadError
documentation:
Incomplete read error. Attributes:

- partial: read bytes string before the end of stream was reached
- expected: total number of expected bytes

classes.name: asyncio.streams.FlowControlMixin
documentation:
Reusable flow control logic for StreamWriter.drain().

This implements the protocol methods pause_writing(),
resume_reading() and connection_lost().  If the subclass overrides
these it must call the super methods.

StreamWriter.drain() must wait for _drain_helper() coroutine.

classes.name: asyncio.streams.StreamReaderProtocol
documentation:
Helper class to adapt between Protocol and StreamReader.

(This is a helper class instead of making StreamReader itself a
Protocol subclass, because the StreamReader has other potential
uses, and to prevent the user of the StreamReader to accidentally
call inappropriate methods of the protocol.)

classes.name: asyncio.streams.StreamWriter

methods.name: asyncio.streams.StreamWriter.drain
documentation:
Flush the write buffer.

The intended use is to write

  w.write(data)
  yield from w.drain()

Wraps a Transport.

This exposes write(), writelines(), [can_]write_eof(),
get_extra_info() and close().  It adds drain() which returns an
optional Future on which you can wait for flow control.  It also
adds a transport property which references the Transport
directly.

methods.name: asyncio.streams.StreamReader._wakeup_waiter
documentation: Wakeup read() or readline() function waiting for data or EOF.

methods.name: asyncio.streams.StreamReader.at_eof
documentation: Return True if the buffer is empty and 'feed_eof' was called.

methods.name: asyncio.streams.StreamReader._wait_for_data
documentation: Wait until feed_data() or feed_eof() is called.

modules.name: asyncio.selectors
documentation:
Selectors module.

This module allows high-level and efficient I/O multiplexing, built upon the
`select` module primitives.

functions.name: asyncio.selectors._fileobj_to_fd
documentation:
Return a file descriptor from a file object.

Parameters:
fileobj -- file object or file descriptor

Returns:
corresponding file descriptor

Raises:
ValueError if the object is invalid

variables.name: asyncio.selectors.SelectorKey
documentation:
Object used to associate a file object to its backing file descriptor,
selected event mask and attached data.

classes.name: asyncio.selectors._SelectorMapping
documentation: Mapping of file objects to selector keys.

classes.name: asyncio.selectors.BaseSelector

methods.name: asyncio.selectors.BaseSelector.register
documentation:
Register a file object.

Parameters:
fileobj -- file object or file descriptor
events  -- events to monitor (bitwise mask of EVENT_READ|EVENT_WRITE)
data    -- attached data

Returns:
SelectorKey instance

Raises:
ValueError if events is invalid
KeyError if fileobj is already registered
OSError if fileobj is closed or otherwise is unacceptable to
        the underlying system call (if a system call is made)

Note:
OSError may or may not be raised

methods.name: asyncio.selectors.BaseSelector.unregister
documentation:
Unregister a file object.

Parameters:
fileobj -- file object or file descriptor

Returns:
SelectorKey instance

Raises:
KeyError if fileobj is not registered

Note:
If fileobj is registered but has since been closed this does
*not* raise OSError (even if the wrapped syscall does)

methods.name: asyncio.selectors.BaseSelector.modify
documentation:
Change a registered file object monitored events or attached data.

Parameters:
fileobj -- file object or file descriptor
events  -- events to monitor (bitwise mask of EVENT_READ|EVENT_WRITE)
data    -- attached data

Returns:
SelectorKey instance

Raises:
Anything that unregister() or register() raises

methods.name: asyncio.selectors.BaseSelector.select
documentation:
Perform the actual selection, until some monitored file objects are
ready or a timeout expires.

Parameters:
timeout -- if timeout > 0, this specifies the maximum wait time, in
           seconds
           if timeout <= 0, the select() call won't block, and will
           report the currently ready file objects
           if timeout is None, select() will block until a monitored
           file object becomes ready

Returns:
list of (key, events) for ready file objects
`events` is a bitwise mask of EVENT_READ|EVENT_WRITE

methods.name: asyncio.selectors.BaseSelector.close
documentation:
Close the selector.

This must be called to make sure that any underlying resource is freed.

methods.name: asyncio.selectors.BaseSelector.get_key
documentation:
Return the key associated to a registered file object.

Returns:
SelectorKey for this file object

methods.name: asyncio.selectors.BaseSelector.get_map
documentation: Return a mapping of file objects to selector keys.

Selector abstract base class.

A selector supports registering file objects to be monitored for specific
I/O events.

A file object is a file descriptor or any object with a `fileno()` method.
An arbitrary object can be attached to the file object, which can be used
for example to store context information, a callback, etc.

A selector can use various implementations (select(), poll(), epoll()...)
depending on the platform. The default `Selector` class uses the most
efficient implementation on the current platform.

classes.name: asyncio.selectors._BaseSelectorImpl

methods.name: asyncio.selectors._BaseSelectorImpl._fileobj_lookup
documentation:
Return a file descriptor from a file object.

This wraps _fileobj_to_fd() to do an exhaustive search in case
the object is invalid but we still have it in our map.  This
is used by unregister() so we can unregister an object that
was previously registered even if it is closed.  It is also
used by _SelectorMapping.

methods.name: asyncio.selectors._BaseSelectorImpl._key_from_fd
documentation:
Return the key associated to a given file descriptor.

Parameters:
fd -- file descriptor

Returns:
corresponding key, or None if not found
Base selector implementation.

classes.name: asyncio.selectors.SelectSelector
documentation: Select-based selector.

classes.name: asyncio.selectors.PollSelector
documentation: Poll-based selector.

classes.name: asyncio.selectors.EpollSelector
documentation: Epoll-based selector.

classes.name: asyncio.selectors.DevpollSelector
documentation: Solaris /dev/poll selector.

classes.name: asyncio.selectors.KqueueSelector
documentation: Kqueue-based selector.

modules.name: asyncio.test_utils
documentation: Utilities shared by tests.

functions.name: asyncio.test_utils.run_once
documentation:
loop.stop() schedules _raise_stop_error()
and run_forever() runs until _raise_stop_error() callback.
this wont work if test waits for some IO events, because
_raise_stop_error() runs before any of io events callbacks.

functions.name: asyncio.test_utils.disable_logger
documentation:
Context manager to disable asyncio logger.

For example, it can be used to ignore warnings in debug mode.

functions.name: asyncio.test_utils.mock_nonblocking_socket
documentation: Create a mock of a non-blocking socket.

classes.name: asyncio.test_utils.TestLoop

methods.name: asyncio.test_utils.TestLoop.advance_time
documentation: Move test time forward.

Loop for unittests.

It manages self time directly.
If something scheduled to be executed later then
on next loop iteration after all ready handlers done
generator passed to __init__ is calling.

Generator should be like this:

    def gen():
        ...
        when = yield ...
        ... = yield time_advance

Value returned by yield is absolute time of next scheduled handler.
Value passed to yield is time advance to move loop's time forward.

classes.name: asyncio.test_utils.MockPattern
documentation:
A regex based str with a fuzzy __eq__.

Use this helper with 'mock.assert_called_with', or anywhere
where a regex comparison between strings is needed.

For instance:
   mock_call.assert_called_with(MockPattern('spam.*ham'))

modules.name: asyncio.constants
documentation: Constants.

modules.name: asyncio.protocols
documentation: Abstract Protocol class.

classes.name: asyncio.protocols.BaseProtocol

methods.name: asyncio.protocols.BaseProtocol.connection_made
documentation:
Called when a connection is made.

The argument is the transport representing the pipe connection.
To receive data, wait for data_received() calls.
When the connection is closed, connection_lost() is called.

methods.name: asyncio.protocols.BaseProtocol.connection_lost
documentation:
Called when the connection is lost or closed.

The argument is an exception object or None (the latter
meaning a regular EOF is received or the connection was
aborted or closed).

methods.name: asyncio.protocols.BaseProtocol.pause_writing
documentation:
Called when the transport's buffer goes over the high-water mark.

Pause and resume calls are paired -- pause_writing() is called
once when the buffer goes strictly over the high-water mark
(even if subsequent writes increases the buffer size even
more), and eventually resume_writing() is called once when the
buffer size reaches the low-water mark.

Note that if the buffer size equals the high-water mark,
pause_writing() is not called -- it must go strictly over.
Conversely, resume_writing() is called when the buffer size is
equal or lower than the low-water mark.  These end conditions
are important to ensure that things go as expected when either
mark is zero.

NOTE: This is the only Protocol callback that is not called
through EventLoop.call_soon() -- if it were, it would have no
effect when it's most needed (when the app keeps writing
without yielding until pause_writing() is called).

methods.name: asyncio.protocols.BaseProtocol.resume_writing
documentation:
Called when the transport's buffer drains below the low-water mark.

See pause_writing() for details.

Common base class for protocol interfaces.

Usually user implements protocols that derived from BaseProtocol
like Protocol or ProcessProtocol.

The only case when BaseProtocol should be implemented directly is
write-only transport like write pipe

classes.name: asyncio.protocols.Protocol

methods.name: asyncio.protocols.Protocol.data_received
documentation:
Called when some data is received.

The argument is a bytes object.

methods.name: asyncio.protocols.Protocol.eof_received
documentation:
Called when the other end calls write_eof() or equivalent.

If this returns a false value (including None), the transport
will close itself.  If it returns a true value, closing the
transport is up to the protocol.

Interface for stream protocol.

The user should implement this interface.  They can inherit from
this class but don't need to.  The implementations here do
nothing (they don't raise exceptions).

When the user wants to requests a transport, they pass a protocol
factory to a utility function (e.g., EventLoop.create_connection()).

When the connection is made successfully, connection_made() is
called with a suitable transport object.  Then data_received()
will be called 0 or more times with data (bytes) received from the
transport; finally, connection_lost() will be called exactly once
with either an exception object or None as an argument.

State machine of calls:

  start -> CM [-> DR*] [-> ER?] -> CL -> end

* CM: connection_made()
* DR: data_received()
* ER: eof_received()
* CL: connection_lost()

classes.name: asyncio.protocols.DatagramProtocol

methods.name: asyncio.protocols.DatagramProtocol.datagram_received
documentation: Called when some datagram is received.

methods.name: asyncio.protocols.DatagramProtocol.error_received
documentation:
Called when a send or receive operation raises an OSError.

(Other than BlockingIOError or InterruptedError.)
Interface for datagram protocol.

classes.name: asyncio.protocols.SubprocessProtocol

methods.name: asyncio.protocols.SubprocessProtocol.pipe_data_received
documentation:
Called when the subprocess writes data into stdout/stderr pipe.

fd is int file descriptor.
data is bytes object.

methods.name: asyncio.protocols.SubprocessProtocol.pipe_connection_lost
documentation:
Called when a file descriptor associated with the child process is
closed.

fd is the int file descriptor that was closed.

methods.name: asyncio.protocols.SubprocessProtocol.process_exited
documentation: Called when subprocess has exited.
Interface for protocol for subprocess calls.

methods.name: asyncio.base_subprocess.BaseSubprocessTransport._wait
documentation:
Wait until the process exit and return the process return code.

This method is a coroutine.

modules.name: asyncio
documentation: The asyncio package, tracking PEP 3156.

modules.name: asyncio.test_support
documentation: Subset of test.support from CPython 3.5, just what we need to run asyncio
test suite. The code is copied from CPython 3.5 to not depend on the test
module because it is rarely installed.
Ignore symbol TEST_HOME_DIR: test_events works without it

functions.name: asyncio.test_support.strip_python_stderr
documentation:
Strip the stderr of a Python process from potential debug output
emitted by the interpreter.

This will typically be run on the result of the communicate() method
of a subprocess.Popen object.

functions.name: asyncio.test_support._assert_python
documentation: Executing the interpreter in a subprocess

functions.name: asyncio.test_support.assert_python_ok
documentation:
Assert that running the interpreter with `args` and optional environment
variables `env_vars` succeeds (rc == 0) and return a (return code, stdout,
stderr) tuple.

If the __cleanenv keyword is set, env_vars is used a fresh environment.

Python is started in isolated mode (command line option -I),
except if the __isolated keyword is set to False.

functions.name: asyncio.test_support.gc_collect
documentation:
Force as many objects as possible to be collected.

In non-CPython implementations of Python, this is needed because timely
deallocation is not guaranteed by the garbage collector.  (Even in CPython
this can be the case in case of reference cycles.)  This means that __del__
methods may be called later than expected and weakrefs may remain alive for
longer than expected.  This function tries its best to force all garbage
objects to disappear.

functions.name: asyncio.test_support._is_ipv6_enabled
documentation: Check whether IPv6 is enabled on this host.

functions.name: asyncio.test_support.find_unused_port
documentation:
Returns an unused port that should be suitable for binding.  This is
achieved by creating a temporary socket with the same family and type as
the 'sock' parameter (default is AF_INET, SOCK_STREAM), and binding it to
the specified host address (defaults to 0.0.0.0) with the port set to 0,
eliciting an unused ephemeral port from the OS.  The temporary socket is
then closed and deleted, and the ephemeral port is returned.

Either this method or bind_port() should be used for any tests where a
server socket needs to be bound to a particular port for the duration of
the test.  Which one to use depends on whether the calling code is creating
a python socket, or if an unused port needs to be provided in a constructor
or passed to an external program (i.e. the -accept argument to openssl's
s_server mode).  Always prefer bind_port() over find_unused_port() where
possible.  Hard coded ports should *NEVER* be used.  As soon as a server
socket is bound to a hard coded port, the ability to run multiple instances
of the test simultaneously on the same host is compromised, which makes the
test a ticking time bomb in a buildbot environment. On Unix buildbots, this
may simply manifest as a failed test, which can be recovered from without
intervention in most cases, but on Windows, the entire python process can
completely and utterly wedge, requiring someone to log in to the buildbot
and manually kill the affected process.

(This is easy to reproduce on Windows, unfortunately, and can be traced to
the SO_REUSEADDR socket option having different semantics on Windows versus
Unix/Linux.  On Unix, you can't have two AF_INET SOCK_STREAM sockets bind,
listen and then accept connections on identical host/ports.  An EADDRINUSE
OSError will be raised at some point (depending on the platform and
the order bind and listen were called on each socket).

However, on Windows, if SO_REUSEADDR is set on the sockets, no EADDRINUSE
will ever be raised when attempting to bind two identical host/ports. When
accept() is called on each socket, the second caller's process will steal
the port from the first caller, leaving them both in an awkwardly wedged
state where they'll no longer respond to any signals or graceful kills, and
must be forcibly killed via OpenProcess()/TerminateProcess().

The solution on Windows is to use the SO_EXCLUSIVEADDRUSE socket option
instead of SO_REUSEADDR, which effectively affords the same semantics as
SO_REUSEADDR on Unix.  Given the propensity of Unix developers in the Open
Source world compared to Windows ones, this is a common mistake.  A quick
look over OpenSSL's 0.9.8g source shows that they use SO_REUSEADDR when
openssl.exe is called with the 's_server' option, for example. See
http://bugs.python.org/issue2550 for more info.  The following site also
has a very thorough description about the implications of both REUSEADDR
and EXCLUSIVEADDRUSE on Windows:
http://msdn2.microsoft.com/en-us/library/ms740621(VS.85).aspx)

XXX: although this approach is a vast improvement on previous attempts to
elicit unused ports, it rests heavily on the assumption that the ephemeral
port returned to us by the OS won't immediately be dished back out to some
other process when we close and delete our temporary socket but before our
calling code has a chance to bind the returned port.  We can deal with this
issue if/when we come across it.

functions.name: asyncio.test_support.bind_port
documentation:
Bind the socket to a free port and return the port number.  Relies on
ephemeral ports in order to ensure we are using an unbound port.  This is
important as many tests may be running simultaneously, especially in a
buildbot environment.  This method raises an exception if the sock.family
is AF_INET and sock.type is SOCK_STREAM, *and* the socket has SO_REUSEADDR
or SO_REUSEPORT set on it.  Tests should *never* set these socket options
for TCP/IP sockets.  The only case for setting these options is testing
multicasting via multiple UDP sockets.

Additionally, if the SO_EXCLUSIVEADDRUSE socket option is available (i.e.
on Windows), it will be set on the socket.  This will prevent anyone else
from bind()'ing to our host/port for the duration of the test.

functions.name: asyncio.test_support.requires_mac_ver
documentation:
Decorator raising SkipTest if the OS is Mac OS X and the OS X
version if less than min_version.

For example, @requires_mac_ver(10, 5) raises SkipTest if the OS X version
is lesser than 10.5.

functions.name: asyncio.test_support._requires_unix_version
documentation:
Decorator raising SkipTest if the OS is `sysname` and the version is
less than `min_version`.

For example, @_requires_unix_version('FreeBSD', (7, 2)) raises SkipTest if
the FreeBSD version is less than 7.2.

functions.name: asyncio.test_support.requires_freebsd_version
documentation:
Decorator raising SkipTest if the OS is FreeBSD and the FreeBSD version
is less than `min_version`.

For example, @requires_freebsd_version(7, 2) raises SkipTest if the FreeBSD
version is less than 7.2.

modules.name: asyncio.unix_events
documentation: Selector event loop for Unix with signal handling.

functions.name: asyncio.unix_events._sighandler_noop
documentation: Dummy signal handler.

classes.name: asyncio.unix_events._UnixSelectorEventLoop

methods.name: asyncio.unix_events._UnixSelectorEventLoop.add_signal_handler
documentation:
Add a handler for a signal.  UNIX only.

Raise ValueError if the signal number is invalid or uncatchable.
Raise RuntimeError if there is a problem setting up the handler.

methods.name: asyncio.unix_events._UnixSelectorEventLoop._handle_signal
documentation: Internal helper that is the actual signal handler.

methods.name: asyncio.unix_events._UnixSelectorEventLoop.remove_signal_handler
documentation:
Remove a handler for a signal.  UNIX only.

Return True if a signal handler was removed, False if not.

methods.name: asyncio.unix_events._UnixSelectorEventLoop._check_signal
documentation:
Internal helper to validate a signal.

Raise ValueError if the signal number is invalid or uncatchable.
Raise RuntimeError if there is a problem setting up the handler.

Unix event loop.

Adds signal handling and UNIX Domain Socket support to SelectorEventLoop.

class_variables.name: asyncio.unix_events._UnixReadPipeTransport.max_size
documentation: max bytes we read in one event loop iteration

classes.name: asyncio.unix_events.AbstractChildWatcher

methods.name: asyncio.unix_events.AbstractChildWatcher.add_child_handler
documentation:
Register a new child handler.

Arrange for callback(pid, returncode, *args) to be called when
process 'pid' terminates. Specifying another callback for the same
process replaces the previous handler.

Note: callback() must be thread-safe.

methods.name: asyncio.unix_events.AbstractChildWatcher.remove_child_handler
documentation:
Removes the handler for process 'pid'.

The function returns True if the handler was successfully removed,
False if there was nothing to remove.

methods.name: asyncio.unix_events.AbstractChildWatcher.attach_loop
documentation:
Attach the watcher to an event loop.

If the watcher was previously attached to an event loop, then it is
first detached before attaching to the new loop.

Note: loop may be None.

methods.name: asyncio.unix_events.AbstractChildWatcher.close
documentation:
Close the watcher.

This must be called to make sure that any underlying resource is freed.

methods.name: asyncio.unix_events.AbstractChildWatcher.__enter__
documentation:
Enter the watcher's context and allow starting new processes

This function must return self

methods.name: asyncio.unix_events.AbstractChildWatcher.__exit__
documentation: Exit the watcher's context

Abstract base class for monitoring child processes.

Objects derived from this class monitor a collection of subprocesses and
report their termination or interruption by a signal.

New callbacks are registered with .add_child_handler(). Starting a new
process must be done within a 'with' block to allow the watcher to suspend
its activity until the new process if fully registered (this is needed to
prevent a race condition in some implementations).

Example:
    with watcher:
        proc = subprocess.Popen("sleep 1")
        watcher.add_child_handler(proc.pid, callback)

Notes:
    Implementations of this class must be thread-safe.

    Since child watcher objects may catch the SIGCHLD signal and call
    waitpid(-1), there should be only one active object per process.

classes.name: asyncio.unix_events.SafeChildWatcher
documentation:
'Safe' child watcher implementation.

This implementation avoids disrupting other code spawning processes by
polling explicitly each process in the SIGCHLD handler instead of calling
os.waitpid(-1).

This is a safe solution but it has a significant overhead when handling a
big number of children (O(n) each time SIGCHLD is raised)

classes.name: asyncio.unix_events.FastChildWatcher
documentation:
'Fast' child watcher implementation.

This implementation reaps every terminated processes by calling
os.waitpid(-1) directly, possibly breaking other code spawning processes
and waiting for their termination.

There is no noticeable overhead when handling a big number of children
(O(1) each time a child terminates).

classes.name: asyncio.unix_events._UnixDefaultEventLoopPolicy

methods.name: asyncio.unix_events._UnixDefaultEventLoopPolicy.set_event_loop
documentation:
Set the event loop.

As a side effect, if a child watcher was set before, then calling
.set_event_loop() from the main thread will call .attach_loop(loop) on
the child watcher.

methods.name: asyncio.unix_events._UnixDefaultEventLoopPolicy.get_child_watcher
documentation:
Get the watcher for child processes.

If not yet set, a SafeChildWatcher object is automatically created.

methods.name: asyncio.unix_events._UnixDefaultEventLoopPolicy.set_child_watcher
documentation: Set the watcher for child processes.
UNIX event loop policy with a watcher for child processes.

modules.name: asyncio.log
documentation: Logging configuration.

functions.name: asyncio.coroutines.has_yield_from_bug
documentation: Check for CPython issue #21209

functions.name: asyncio.coroutines.coroutine
documentation:
Decorator to mark coroutines.

If the coroutine is not yielded from before it is destroyed,
an error message is logged.

functions.name: asyncio.coroutines.iscoroutinefunction
documentation: Return True if func is a decorated coroutine function.

functions.name: asyncio.coroutines.iscoroutine
documentation: Return True if obj is a coroutine object.

methods.name: asyncio.coroutines.CoroWrapper.__init__
documentation: Wrapper for coroutine object in _DEBUG mode.

methods.name: asyncio.coroutines.CoroWrapper.send
documentation: For for CPython issue #21209: using "yield from" and a custom
generator, generator.send(tuple) unpacks the tuple instead of passing
the tuple unchanged. Check if the caller is a generator using "yield
from" to decide if the parameter should be unpacked or not.

classes.name: asyncio.subprocess.SubprocessStreamProtocol
documentation: Like StreamReaderProtocol, but for a subprocess.

methods.name: asyncio.subprocess.Process.wait
documentation:
Wait until the process exit and return the process return code.

This method is a coroutine.

classes.name: asyncio.sslproto._SSLPipe

methods.name: asyncio.sslproto._SSLPipe.__init__
documentation:
The *context* argument specifies the ssl.SSLContext to use.

The *server_side* argument indicates whether this is a server side or
client side transport.

The optional *server_hostname* argument can be used to specify the
hostname you are connecting to. You may only specify this parameter if
the _ssl module supports Server Name Indication (SNI).

methods.name: asyncio.sslproto._SSLPipe.context
documentation: The SSL context passed to the constructor.

methods.name: asyncio.sslproto._SSLPipe.ssl_object
documentation:
The internal ssl.SSLObject instance.

Return None if the pipe is not wrapped.

methods.name: asyncio.sslproto._SSLPipe.need_ssldata
documentation:
Whether more record level data is needed to complete a handshake
that is currently in progress.

methods.name: asyncio.sslproto._SSLPipe.wrapped
documentation:
Whether a security layer is currently in effect.

Return False during handshake.

methods.name: asyncio.sslproto._SSLPipe.do_handshake
documentation:
Start the SSL handshake.

Return a list of ssldata. A ssldata element is a list of buffers

The optional *callback* argument can be used to install a callback that
will be called when the handshake is complete. The callback will be
called with None if successful, else an exception instance.

methods.name: asyncio.sslproto._SSLPipe.shutdown
documentation:
Start the SSL shutdown sequence.

Return a list of ssldata. A ssldata element is a list of buffers

The optional *callback* argument can be used to install a callback that
will be called when the shutdown is complete. The callback will be
called without arguments.

methods.name: asyncio.sslproto._SSLPipe.feed_eof
documentation:
Send a potentially "ragged" EOF.

This method will raise an SSL_ERROR_EOF exception if the EOF is
unexpected.

methods.name: asyncio.sslproto._SSLPipe.feed_ssldata
documentation:
Feed SSL record level data into the pipe.

The data must be a bytes instance. It is OK to send an empty bytes
instance. This can be used to get ssldata for a handshake initiated by
this endpoint.

Return a (ssldata, appdata) tuple. The ssldata element is a list of
buffers containing SSL data that needs to be sent to the remote SSL.

The appdata element is a list of buffers containing plaintext data that
needs to be forwarded to the application. The appdata list may contain
an empty buffer indicating an SSL "close_notify" alert. This alert must
be acknowledged by calling shutdown().

methods.name: asyncio.sslproto._SSLPipe.feed_appdata
documentation:
Feed plaintext data into the pipe.

Return an (ssldata, offset) tuple. The ssldata element is a list of
buffers containing record level data that needs to be sent to the
remote SSL instance. The offset is the number of plaintext bytes that
were processed, which may be less than the length of data.

NOTE: In case of short writes, this call MUST be retried with the SAME
buffer passed into the *data* argument (i.e. the id() must be the
same). This is an OpenSSL requirement. A further particularity is that
a short write will always have offset == 0, because the _ssl module
does not enable partial writes. And even though the offset is zero,
there will still be encrypted data in ssldata.

class_variables.name: asyncio.sslproto._SSLPipe.max_size
documentation: Buffer size passed to read()

An SSL "Pipe".

An SSL pipe allows you to communicate with an SSL/TLS protocol instance
through memory buffers. It can be used to implement a security layer for an
existing connection where you don't have access to the connection's file
descriptor, or for some reason you don't want to use it.

An SSL pipe can be in "wrapped" and "unwrapped" mode. In unwrapped mode,
data is passed through untransformed. In wrapped mode, application level
data is encrypted to SSL record level data and vice versa. The SSL record
level is the lowest level in the SSL protocol suite and is what travels
as-is over the wire.

An SslPipe initially is in "unwrapped" mode. To start SSL, call
do_handshake(). To shutdown SSL again, call unwrap().

methods.name: asyncio.sslproto._SSLProtocolTransport.get_extra_info
documentation: Get optional transport information.

methods.name: asyncio.sslproto._SSLProtocolTransport.close
documentation:
Close the transport.

Buffered data will be flushed asynchronously.  No more data
will be received.  After all buffered data is flushed, the
protocol's connection_lost() method will (eventually) called
with None as its argument.

methods.name: asyncio.sslproto._SSLProtocolTransport.pause_reading
documentation:
Pause the receiving end.

No data will be passed to the protocol's data_received()
method until resume_reading() is called.

methods.name: asyncio.sslproto._SSLProtocolTransport.resume_reading
documentation:
Resume the receiving end.

Data received will once again be passed to the protocol's
data_received() method.

methods.name: asyncio.sslproto._SSLProtocolTransport.set_write_buffer_limits
documentation:
Set the high- and low-water limits for write flow control.

These two values control when to call the protocol's
pause_writing() and resume_writing() methods.  If specified,
the low-water limit must be less than or equal to the
high-water limit.  Neither value can be negative.

The defaults are implementation-specific.  If only the
high-water limit is given, the low-water limit defaults to a
implementation-specific value less than or equal to the
high-water limit.  Setting high to zero forces low to zero as
well, and causes pause_writing() to be called whenever the
buffer becomes non-empty.  Setting low to zero causes
resume_writing() to be called only once the buffer is empty.
Use of zero for either limit is generally sub-optimal as it
reduces opportunities for doing I/O and computation
concurrently.

methods.name: asyncio.sslproto._SSLProtocolTransport.get_write_buffer_size
documentation: Return the current size of the write buffer.

methods.name: asyncio.sslproto._SSLProtocolTransport.write
documentation:
Write some data bytes to the transport.

This does not block; it buffers the data and arranges for it
to be sent out asynchronously.

methods.name: asyncio.sslproto._SSLProtocolTransport.can_write_eof
documentation: Return True if this transport supports write_eof(), False if not.

methods.name: asyncio.sslproto._SSLProtocolTransport.abort
documentation:
Close the transport immediately.

Buffered data will be lost.  No more data will be received.
The protocol's connection_lost() method will (eventually) be
called with None as its argument.

classes.name: asyncio.sslproto.SSLProtocol

methods.name: asyncio.sslproto.SSLProtocol.connection_made
documentation:
Called when the low-level connection is made.

Start the SSL handshake.

methods.name: asyncio.sslproto.SSLProtocol.connection_lost
documentation:
Called when the low-level connection is lost or closed.

The argument is an exception object or None (the latter
meaning a regular EOF is received or the connection was
aborted or closed).

methods.name: asyncio.sslproto.SSLProtocol.pause_writing
documentation:
Called when the low-level transport's buffer goes over
the high-water mark.

methods.name: asyncio.sslproto.SSLProtocol.resume_writing
documentation:
Called when the low-level transport's buffer drains below
the low-water mark.

methods.name: asyncio.sslproto.SSLProtocol.data_received
documentation:
Called when some SSL data is received.

The argument is a bytes object.

methods.name: asyncio.sslproto.SSLProtocol.eof_received
documentation:
Called when the other end of the low-level stream
is half-closed.

If this returns a false value (including None), the transport
will close itself.  If it returns a true value, closing the
transport is up to the protocol.

SSL protocol.

Implementation of SSL on top of a socket using incoming and outgoing
buffers which are ssl.MemoryBIO objects.

modules.name: asyncio.proactor_events
documentation:
Event loop using a proactor and related classes.

A proactor is a "notify-on-completion" multiplexer.  Currently a
proactor is only implemented on Windows with IOCP.

classes.name: asyncio.proactor_events._ProactorBasePipeTransport
documentation: Base class for pipe and socket transports.

classes.name: asyncio.proactor_events._ProactorReadPipeTransport
documentation: Transport for read pipes.

classes.name: asyncio.proactor_events._ProactorBaseWritePipeTransport
documentation: Transport for write pipes.

classes.name: asyncio.proactor_events._ProactorDuplexPipeTransport
documentation: Transport for duplex pipes.

classes.name: asyncio.proactor_events._ProactorSocketTransport
documentation: Transport for connected sockets.

modules.name: asyncio.windows_events
documentation:
Failed to index ``asyncio/windows_events.py`` due to ``Syntax Error @ 492:15.
parser error: error at 491:14: expected one of !=, %, &, (, *, **, +, ,, -, /, //, :, ;, <, <<, <=, =, ==, >, >=, >>, @, NEWLINE, [, ^, and, if, in, is, not, or, |

        return future
              ^``

modules.name: asyncio.transports
documentation: Abstract Transport class.

classes.name: asyncio.transports.BaseTransport

methods.name: asyncio.transports.BaseTransport.get_extra_info
documentation: Get optional transport information.

methods.name: asyncio.transports.BaseTransport.close
documentation:
Close the transport.

Buffered data will be flushed asynchronously.  No more data
will be received.  After all buffered data is flushed, the
protocol's connection_lost() method will (eventually) called
with None as its argument.
Base class for transports.

classes.name: asyncio.transports.ReadTransport

methods.name: asyncio.transports.ReadTransport.pause_reading
documentation:
Pause the receiving end.

No data will be passed to the protocol's data_received()
method until resume_reading() is called.

methods.name: asyncio.transports.ReadTransport.resume_reading
documentation:
Resume the receiving end.

Data received will once again be passed to the protocol's
data_received() method.
Interface for read-only transports.

classes.name: asyncio.transports.WriteTransport

methods.name: asyncio.transports.WriteTransport.set_write_buffer_limits
documentation:
Set the high- and low-water limits for write flow control.

These two values control when to call the protocol's
pause_writing() and resume_writing() methods.  If specified,
the low-water limit must be less than or equal to the
high-water limit.  Neither value can be negative.

The defaults are implementation-specific.  If only the
high-water limit is given, the low-water limit defaults to a
implementation-specific value less than or equal to the
high-water limit.  Setting high to zero forces low to zero as
well, and causes pause_writing() to be called whenever the
buffer becomes non-empty.  Setting low to zero causes
resume_writing() to be called only once the buffer is empty.
Use of zero for either limit is generally sub-optimal as it
reduces opportunities for doing I/O and computation
concurrently.

methods.name: asyncio.transports.WriteTransport.get_write_buffer_size
documentation: Return the current size of the write buffer.

methods.name: asyncio.transports.WriteTransport.write
documentation:
Write some data bytes to the transport.

This does not block; it buffers the data and arranges for it
to be sent out asynchronously.

methods.name: asyncio.transports.WriteTransport.writelines
documentation:
Write a list (or any iterable) of data bytes to the transport.

The default implementation concatenates the arguments and
calls write() on the result.

methods.name: asyncio.transports.WriteTransport.write_eof
documentation:
Close the write end after flushing buffered data.

(This is like typing ^D into a UNIX program reading from stdin.)

Data may still be received.

methods.name: asyncio.transports.WriteTransport.can_write_eof
documentation: Return True if this transport supports write_eof(), False if not.

methods.name: asyncio.transports.WriteTransport.abort
documentation:
Close the transport immediately.

Buffered data will be lost.  No more data will be received.
The protocol's connection_lost() method will (eventually) be
called with None as its argument.
Interface for write-only transports.

classes.name: asyncio.transports.Transport
documentation:
Interface representing a bidirectional transport.

There may be several implementations, but typically, the user does
not implement new transports; rather, the platform provides some
useful transports that are implemented using the platform's best
practices.

The user never instantiates a transport directly; they call a
utility function, passing it a protocol factory and other
information necessary to create the transport and protocol.  (E.g.
EventLoop.create_connection() or EventLoop.create_server().)

The utility function will asynchronously create a transport and a
protocol and hook them up by calling the protocol's
connection_made() method, passing it the transport.

The implementation here raises NotImplemented for every method
except writelines(), which calls write() in a loop.

classes.name: asyncio.transports.DatagramTransport

methods.name: asyncio.transports.DatagramTransport.sendto
documentation:
Send data to the transport.

This does not block; it buffers the data and arranges for it
to be sent out asynchronously.
addr is target socket address.
If addr is None use target address pointed on transport creation.

methods.name: asyncio.transports.DatagramTransport.abort
documentation:
Close the transport immediately.

Buffered data will be lost.  No more data will be received.
The protocol's connection_lost() method will (eventually) be
called with None as its argument.
Interface for datagram (UDP) transports.

methods.name: asyncio.transports.SubprocessTransport.get_pid
documentation: Get subprocess id.

methods.name: asyncio.transports.SubprocessTransport.get_returncode
documentation:
Get subprocess returncode.

See also
http://docs.python.org/3/library/subprocess#subprocess.Popen.returncode

methods.name: asyncio.transports.SubprocessTransport.get_pipe_transport
documentation: Get transport for pipe with number fd.

methods.name: asyncio.transports.SubprocessTransport.send_signal
documentation:
Send signal to subprocess.

See also:
docs.python.org/3/library/subprocess#subprocess.Popen.send_signal

methods.name: asyncio.transports.SubprocessTransport.terminate
documentation:
Stop the subprocess.

Alias for close() method.

On Posix OSs the method sends SIGTERM to the subprocess.
On Windows the Win32 API function TerminateProcess()
 is called to stop the subprocess.

See also:
http://docs.python.org/3/library/subprocess#subprocess.Popen.terminate

methods.name: asyncio.transports.SubprocessTransport.kill
documentation:
Kill the subprocess.

On Posix OSs the function sends SIGKILL to the subprocess.
On Windows kill() is an alias for terminate().

See also:
http://docs.python.org/3/library/subprocess#subprocess.Popen.kill

classes.name: asyncio.transports._FlowControlMixin
documentation:
All the logic for (write) flow control in a mix-in base class.

The subclass must implement get_write_buffer_size().  It must call
_maybe_pause_protocol() whenever the write buffer size increases,
and _maybe_resume_protocol() whenever it decreases.  It may also
override set_write_buffer_limits() (e.g. to specify different
defaults).

The subclass constructor must call super().__init__(extra).  This
will call set_write_buffer_limits().

The user may call set_write_buffer_limits() and
get_write_buffer_size(), and their protocol's pause_writing() and
resume_writing() may be called.

modules.name: asyncio.tasks
documentation:
Failed to index ``asyncio/tasks.py`` due to ``Syntax Error @ 331:1.
parser error: error at 330:15: expected one of *, **, +, -, ..., AWAIT, False, NUMBER, None, True, lambda, not, }, ~

    fs = {async(f, loop=loop) for f in set(fs)}
                                              ^``

modules.name: asyncio.selector_events
documentation:
Event loop using a selector and related classes.

A selector is a "notify-when-ready" multiplexer.  For a subclass which
also includes support for signal handling, see the unix_events sub-module.

classes.name: asyncio.selector_events.BaseSelectorEventLoop

methods.name: asyncio.selector_events.BaseSelectorEventLoop.add_reader
documentation: Add a reader callback.

methods.name: asyncio.selector_events.BaseSelectorEventLoop.remove_reader
documentation: Remove a reader callback.

methods.name: asyncio.selector_events.BaseSelectorEventLoop.add_writer
documentation: Add a writer callback..

methods.name: asyncio.selector_events.BaseSelectorEventLoop.remove_writer
documentation: Remove a writer callback.

methods.name: asyncio.selector_events.BaseSelectorEventLoop.sock_recv
documentation:
Receive data from the socket.

The return value is a bytes object representing the data received.
The maximum amount of data to be received at once is specified by
nbytes.

This method is a coroutine.

methods.name: asyncio.selector_events.BaseSelectorEventLoop.sock_sendall
documentation:
Send data to the socket.

The socket must be connected to a remote socket. This method continues
to send data from data until either all data has been sent or an
error occurs. None is returned on success. On error, an exception is
raised, and there is no way to determine how much data, if any, was
successfully processed by the receiving end of the connection.

This method is a coroutine.

methods.name: asyncio.selector_events.BaseSelectorEventLoop.sock_connect
documentation:
Connect to a remote socket at address.

The address must be already resolved to avoid the trap of hanging the
entire event loop when the address requires doing a DNS lookup. For
example, it must be an IP address, not an hostname, for AF_INET and
AF_INET6 address families. Use getaddrinfo() to resolve the hostname
asynchronously.

This method is a coroutine.

methods.name: asyncio.selector_events.BaseSelectorEventLoop.sock_accept
documentation:
Accept a connection.

The socket must be bound to an address and listening for connections.
The return value is a pair (conn, address) where conn is a new socket
object usable to send and receive data on the connection, and address
is the address bound to the socket on the other end of the connection.

This method is a coroutine.

Selector event loop.

See events.EventLoop for API specification.

class_variables.name: asyncio.selector_events._SelectorTransport.max_size
documentation: Buffer size passed to recv().

class_variables.name: asyncio.selector_events._SelectorTransport._buffer_factory
documentation: Constructs initial value for self._buffer.

modules.name: asyncio.windows_utils
documentation: Various Windows specific bits and pieces

functions.name: asyncio.windows_utils.socketpair
documentation:
A socket pair usable as a self-pipe, for Windows.

Origin: https://gist.github.com/4325783, by Geert Jansen.
Public domain.
Replacement for socket.socketpair()

functions.name: asyncio.windows_utils.pipe
documentation: Like os.pipe() but with overlapped support and using handles not fds.
Replacement for os.pipe() using handles instead of fds

classes.name: asyncio.windows_utils.PipeHandle
documentation:
Wrapper for an overlapped pipe handle which is vaguely file-object like.

The IOCP event loop can use these instead of socket objects.
Wrapper for a pipe handle

classes.name: asyncio.windows_utils.Popen
documentation:
Replacement for subprocess.Popen using overlapped pipe handles.

The stdin, stdout, stderr are None or instances of PipeHandle.
Replacement for subprocess.Popen using overlapped pipe handles

modules.name: asyncio.queues
documentation: Queues

classes.name: asyncio.queues.QueueEmpty
documentation:
Exception raised when Queue.get_nowait() is called on a Queue object
which is empty.

classes.name: asyncio.queues.QueueFull
documentation:
Exception raised when the Queue.put_nowait() method is called on a Queue
object which is full.

classes.name: asyncio.queues.Queue

methods.name: asyncio.queues.Queue.qsize
documentation: Number of items in the queue.

methods.name: asyncio.queues.Queue.maxsize
documentation: Number of items allowed in the queue.

methods.name: asyncio.queues.Queue.empty
documentation: Return True if the queue is empty, False otherwise.

methods.name: asyncio.queues.Queue.full
documentation:
Return True if there are maxsize items in the queue.

Note: if the Queue was initialized with maxsize=0 (the default),
then full() is never True.

methods.name: asyncio.queues.Queue.put
documentation:
Put an item into the queue.

Put an item into the queue. If the queue is full, wait until a free
slot is available before adding item.

This method is a coroutine.

methods.name: asyncio.queues.Queue.put_nowait
documentation:
Put an item into the queue without blocking.

If no free slot is immediately available, raise QueueFull.

methods.name: asyncio.queues.Queue.get
documentation:
Remove and return an item from the queue.

If queue is empty, wait until an item is available.

This method is a coroutine.

methods.name: asyncio.queues.Queue.get_nowait
documentation:
Remove and return an item from the queue.

Return an item if one is immediately available, else raise QueueEmpty.

A queue, useful for coordinating producer and consumer coroutines.

If maxsize is less than or equal to zero, the queue size is infinite. If it
is an integer greater than 0, then "yield from put()" will block when the
queue reaches maxsize, until an item is removed by get().

Unlike the standard library Queue, you can reliably know this Queue's size
with qsize(), since your single-threaded asyncio application won't be
interrupted between calling qsize() and doing an operation on the Queue.

classes.name: asyncio.queues.PriorityQueue
documentation:
A subclass of Queue; retrieves entries in priority order (lowest first).

Entries are typically tuples of the form: (priority number, data).

classes.name: asyncio.queues.LifoQueue
documentation: A subclass of Queue that retrieves most recently added entries first.

classes.name: asyncio.queues.JoinableQueue

methods.name: asyncio.queues.JoinableQueue.task_done
documentation:
Indicate that a formerly enqueued task is complete.

Used by queue consumers. For each get() used to fetch a task,
a subsequent call to task_done() tells the queue that the processing
on the task is complete.

If a join() is currently blocking, it will resume when all items have
been processed (meaning that a task_done() call was received for every
item that had been put() into the queue).

Raises ValueError if called more times than there were items placed in
the queue.

methods.name: asyncio.queues.JoinableQueue.join
documentation:
Block until all items in the queue have been gotten and processed.

The count of unfinished tasks goes up whenever an item is added to the
queue. The count goes down whenever a consumer thread calls task_done()
to indicate that the item was retrieved and all work on it is complete.
When the count of unfinished tasks drops to zero, join() unblocks.
A subclass of Queue with task_done() and join() methods.
